coredata.dc:title: A new application of papain: As a peroxidase-like catalyst for fluorometric detection of uric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Wenya
Zhao, Wuyong
Yi, Sijing
Gao, Wenmei
Zhang, Jiangang
Zhao, Jinzhong
Liu, Xiaoxia
coredata.dc:description: Papain, as a classical cysteine protease, has been widely used in the food, pharmaceutical, chemical, and cosmetic fields. However, there are few information about the peroxidase-like activity of papain catalyzed substrate to produce fluorescence. In this study, we found that papain can catalyze H2O2 to convert o-phenylenediamine (OPD), and generate fluorescence emission at 550 nm under 430 nm excitation. Based on this foundation, we report a papain/OPD/H2O2 system for fluorescence detection of uric acid. The method exhibits a wide linear range of 10–1000 μM with a limit-of-detection of 4.6 μM, and has been successfully used to detect uric acid in human serum. This study paves the way for the application of papain as catalyst for fluorescence detection of different target biomolecules, such as cholesterol, glucose, lactate, for which H2O2 is a product of oxidoreductase enzymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Papain
Peroxidase-like activity
Fluorescence
Uric acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence studies of the papain/OPD/H2O2 system and standard DAP solution (0.1 mM). The insert photographs are the product solution under sunlight (left) and 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (right) respectively.

(A) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity; (B) Changes of fluorescence intensity with time under different papain concentrations; (C) Temperature effects on papain and HRP stability(other conditions: 0.25 mM OPD, 0.25 mM H2O2).
(A) Effect of OPD concentration on the fluorescence intensity; (B) Double-reciprocal plots of initial velocity vs OPD concentration.

Fluorescence intensity (A) and absorbance spectra (C) with increasing concentrations of UA. (B) Linear fitting curves of fluorescence intensity at 550 nm vs UA concentration. (D) Linear fitting curve of absorbance at 420 nm vs. UA concentration.
Selectivity of the proposed method for UA detection (The final concentrations of the interference and UA were 500 μM and 200 μM).
Schematic illustration of determination of uric acid.
Comparison of different methods for uric acid detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CQD, carbon quantum dots; GQDs, gold quantum dots; BSA, bovine serum albumin; GNCs, gold nanoclusters; MQDs, MXenes quantum dots; NPs, nanoparticles; Au/AgNCs, gold/silver nanoclusters.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of UA in human serum.

head.ce:title: A new application of papain: As a peroxidase-like catalyst for fluorometric detection of uric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Teaching, Shanxi Agricultural University, Jinzhong, Shanxi 030801, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Basic Teaching, Shanxi Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Basic Teaching, Shanxi Agricultural University, Jinzhong, Shanxi 030801, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rehabilitation Department, Shanxi Bethune Hospital, Taiyuan, Shanxi 030032, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Rehabilitation Department, Shanxi Bethune Hospital, Taiyuan, Shanxi 030032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Papain, as a classical cysteine protease, has been widely used in the food, pharmaceutical, chemical, and cosmetic fields. However, there are few information about the peroxidase-like activity of papain catalyzed substrate to produce fluorescence. In this study, we found that papain can catalyze H2O2 to convert o-phenylenediamine (OPD), and generate fluorescence emission at 550 nm under 430 nm excitation. Based on this foundation, we report a papain/OPD/H2O2 system for fluorescence detection of uric acid. The method exhibits a wide linear range of 10–1000 μM with a limit-of-detection of 4.6 μM, and has been successfully used to detect uric acid in human serum. This study paves the way for the application of papain as catalyst for fluorescence detection of different target biomolecules, such as cholesterol, glucose, lactate, for which H2O2 is a product of oxidoreductase enzymes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Papain (EC 3.4.22.2), as a classical cysteine protease found in the latex of Carica papaya, consisting of 212 amino acid residues and the molecular weight is 23.35 kDa [1,2]. Due to its excellent performance, such as high enzymatic activity, wide pH range of action, high-temperature stability, low cost, and non-toxicity, papain has gained widespread interest in various fields. In food industry, it has been used to produce bio-peptides by hydrolysis of food proteins [3,4] and it is listed among the enzyme preparations recognized by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as GRAS (generally recognized as safe) substance. In this way, papain was used as a food additive in meat tenderization, baking and milk industry [5,6]. In biomedical clinical application, papain acts as an enzyme with bactericidal, anti-inflammatory characteristics and promotion of debridement, which accelerates tissue regeneration. It is already available on the market as an ointment and gels for wound healing [7–9]. In addition, papain as one of the potential active ingredients, can promote local cell multiplication by stimulating the production of cytokines, reducing the scar, which have a favorable esthetic effect [10].

In the field of chemistry, papain has been used as a biosensor to measure cystatin C and chlorothalonil due to their inhibitive effect on papain [11,12] When the N-terminus of cystatin C is binds to the catalytic center of papain, the activity of papain is inhibited. Papain also possess peroxidase activities similar to horseradish peroxidase (HRP), and can catalyze oxidation reactions with a color change using 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB)/H2O2 in colorimetric glucose determination [13]. Papain has also been used as an effective capping and reducing agent for synthesis of metal nanoclusters (NCs) [14–20]. The papain-stabilized metal NCs were employed as probes for detection of Cu2+ [17], H2O2 [18], dopamine [19], and D-penicillamine [20] based on the fluorescence properties of the metal NCs. However, there are few information about the catalytic activity of papain on the substrate to generate fluorescence.

In this study, we show that papain could catalyze the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) by H2O2 to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), which exhibited an obvious yellow fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity was related to several parameters, such as the pH of reaction buffer, incubation time, concentration of OPD, H2O2 and papain. Uric acid (UA) is the final product of purine metabolism in humans and great apes. Normally, serum UA levels in healthy adults range from 90 to 360 μM for women and 150–420 μM for men [21–23]. A higher or a lower UA levels can lead to several pathological symptoms such as arthritis, gout and kidney related disease [24,25]. Therefore, we applied the papain/OPD/H2O2 system to fluorometric detection of UA by monitoring the concentration of H2O2, the product of the uric acid/O2 reaction catalyzed by uricase ( Scheme 1). This method exhibited excellent selectivity and a wide linear range for UA determination, indicating the great potential applications in biosensing assays. The peroxidase-like activities of papain may be useful in biomolecular quantitation, such as the determination of glucose, cholesterol, etc., in which H2O2 is a product of oxidoreductase enzymes.

Uric acid was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (America). Uricase was obtained from Yuanye Bio-Technology (88 U/mg, Shanghai, China). Papain, OPD, KCl, glucose, urea, glycine, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) and alcohol were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). The chemicals in this study were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure water from a Milli-Q automatic ultrapure water system was used throughout the experiments.
The fluorescence emission spectra were obtained with a RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and the slit widths of excitation and emission were both set as 5.0 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characterization of the papain/OPD/H2O2 system
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of papain was studied using OPD as the substrate in the presence of H2O2. In brief, 100 μL of 10 mM OPD and 100 μL of 10 mM H2O2 were orderly added into 3700 μL of NaAc–HAc buffer (pH 5.5, 20 mM). Then, 100 μL of 2 mg/mL papain was added to initiate the enzyme reaction. After 5 min, the fluorescence signals were detected by a spectrophotometer. Control experiments were operated in the absence of H2O2 or papain under the same experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the catalytic reaction conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the analytical performance, several reaction conditions were investigated, including the pH (4.0–7.0), the concentration of OPD and H2O2, and the amount of papain on the activity of the papain/OPD/H2O2 system.

UA detection was performed as follows: (1) uricase and UA were both dissolved in 20 mM borate buffer (pH 8.5). Then, 10 μL of 1 mg/mL uricase and 90 μL of UA of different concentration were incubated for 10 min at room temperature to produce H2O2; (2) 100 μL of 2.0 mg/mL papain, 100 μL of 10 mM OPD and 3700 μL of NaAc–HAc buffer (20 mM, pH 5.5) were added successively to the above mixture; (3) after a 15 min-incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The feasibility and selectivity of the proposed method was investigated by adding different potential interfering substances (such as glucose, amino acid, BSA, urea, metal ions) instead of UA. The final concentrations of the interference and UA were 500 μM and 200 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of UA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the practical application of the proposed method, we analyzed the UA concentrations in human serum samples. The human blood sample from volunteer was originally collected in a heparin anticoagulated tube from a local hospital with informed consent from human subject, then, centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min to collect the supernatant. Then various amounts of UA were spiked to the serum sample. It is noteworthy that serum samples was mixed with a series of solution, including uricase, borate buffer, OPD, H2O2, HAc-NaAc buffer before the fluorescence detection, as a result of 40 times diluted, avoiding the interference of endogenous fluorescent substances in the blood.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characterization of the papain/OPD/H2O2 system
ce:section.ce:para: Papain could catalyze the oxidation of OPD by H2O2 to form DAP, exhibiting visible yellow color and yellow fluorescence because of its peroxidase-like activity. Fluorescence studies are shown in Fig. 1, the product exhibited maximum emission at 550 nm under excitation at 430 nm. In contrast, there were almost no signals with either OPD/H2O2 or OPD/papain. In addition, we performed validation test by analyzing the fluorescence of DAP (the dotted lines in Fig. 1). The excitation wavelength and emission wavelength are the same as those of the product, indicating there is DAP generation in papain/OPD/H2O2 reaction system. The insert photographs are the product solution under sunlight (left) and 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (right) respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the catalytic reaction conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain better performance of the established method, we optimized several parameters that may affect the fluorescence intensity of the papain/OPD/H2O2 system, including pH, temperature, incubation time and concentration of papain. We measured the fluorescence intensity while the pH is increased from 4.0 to 7.0 ( Fig. 2A). Similar to other enzyme mimics, the acidic pH condition was more suitable and the maximum fluorescence intensity is obtained at pH 5.5. Fig. 2B shows the time-dependent fluorescence intensity at different papain concentrations. As the papain concentration increased, the reaction rate gradually increased, and less time was required for complete reaction, indicating the reaction was catalyzed by papain and reaction rate depends on the papain concentration. Considering the catalytic efficiency and analysis time, we choose 0.05 mg/mL papain for 15 min in the following experiments. As shown in Fig. 2C, there was no significant change in papain activity in the range of 25–50 ℃, so the following experiments were operating at room temperature. We compared the resistance to heat of papain and HRP, papain retain 67 % activity even at temperature as high as 60 ℃ while HRP retain 45 %, which is consistent with the literature report [26–29]. The results indicate papain has better thermal stability compared to HRP. We also optimized the concentration of uricase due to it affects the amount of H2O2 produced, which in turn affects the detection sensitivity. As shown in Fig. S1A, the fluorescence intensity first increases and then reached a stable state with increasing uricase. We choose 1.0 mg/mL uricase in the following experiments. As presented in Fig. S1B, with the increase of incubation time, the fluorescence intensity also increased and basically reached a stable state at 20 min. To ensure maximum fluorescence signal, 20 min was chosen as the incubation time.

Fig. 3 shows the fluorescence intensity at different OPD concentrations (0.1–4.0 mM) and double-reciprocal curves. The Km (the Michaelis constant) and Vmax (the maximal reaction velocity) values of OPD were obtained from the slope and intercept of the Lineweaver-Burk equation:
1 V
=
K m
V
max
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V
max
where V is the initial velocity, and [S] represents the substrate concentration). The Km and Vmax values were calculated to be 0.84 ± 0.06 mM and (12.08 ± 1.15) × 10−8 M/s, respectively. As we know, Km indicates the affinity between the enzyme and substrate, a small Km value means the strong affinity. Our results show that the Km value of papain towards OPD was close to that of HRP (0.59 mM) [30,31], suggesting that papain can also act as an efficient peroxidase mimic. Table S1 shows a comparison of the kinetic parameters between papain and other peroxidase mimics.

Uric acid detection is based on the uricase/papain enzyme-linked reaction, detection mechanism is as follows:
Uric
acid +
O
2
⟶
uricase
allantoin +
H
2
O
2
+ C
O
2
OPD +
H
2
O
2
⟶
papain
DAP +
H
2
O

Therefore, the applicability of the proposed method for detecting UA relies on the H2O2-concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity. Therefore, we studied the curves of the fluorescence intensity vs the concentration of H2O2 under the optimized conditions. As seen in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity at 550 nm and absorbance at 420 nm increased with increasing concentration of H2O2. Furthermore, a good linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence intensity and UA concentration from 10 to 1000 μM ( Fig. 4A and B) with a regression equation y = 0.6105x + 62.89 (R2 = 0.9924). Apart from the fluorescence intensity result, Fig. 4C shows the colorimetric result. With the increase of UA concentration, the absorbance at 420 nm increases gradually. The absorbance is linearly related to UA in the 50–1500 μΜ concentration range with a regression equation y = 0.00122x + 0.18945(R2 = 0.9908). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on 3Sb/slope (Sb is the standard deviation of the blank samples and slope refers to the slope of linear fitting). The LODs of fluorescent and colorimetric methods were calculated to be 4.6 and 21 μM, respectively. Colorimetric methods has a higher LOD since DAP posses a low molar absorption coefficient (1.67 ×10−4 M−1 cm−1) [30]. The results are compared with previously reported fluorimetric methods [32–40] in Table 1. These data show that the linear range of our method is wider than or comparable to that of previous methods. The wide linear range contains the upper limit of UA values in healthy human, avoiding the need for dilution before detection.

To demonstrate the selectivity of the proposed method, some possible interfering chemicals coexisting in human serum such as glucose, amino acids, BSA, urea, metal ions were individually added to the catalytic solution. Fig. 5 shows that only UA caused a significant increase in fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity changed slightly after adding various interfering substances, and the fluorescence intensity ratio ranged from 92.8% to 109.9 %. The results indicates interference presented negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity, even when their concentrations were 2.5 times higher than UA, indicating the method had an excellent selectivity toward UA determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of UA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the reliability, the developed method was used to detect UA in real serum samples. The result we measured (414.6 μM) was close to the value (396.4 μM) measured in a local hospital, the relative error is 4.6 %. As listed in the Table 2, the recovery ranged from 99.6 % to 102.2 % in the spiked samples, and the RSD (relative standard deviations) are less than 1.72 % (n = 3). Therefore, the results displayed the good potential of this method for UA measurement in real samples.

In summary, we studied the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of papain, which could catalyze the oxidation of OPD by H2O2 to produce fluorescence. Based on this foundation, we developed a simple novel fluorometric method for detection of UA in human serum. The method exhibits a wide detection range of 10–1000 μM as well as a high selectivity. This good performance could be of potential value in a variety of biosensing assays. There is no denying that the catalytic efficiency of papain is lower than HRP. Compared to HRP, the most widely used peroxidase, the catalytic activity of papain is lower. In this work, a higher papain concentration was used. We look forward to enhance catalytic efficiency of papain via mimicking the architecture of the active site in HRP and further extending the application of papain as a catalyst for fluorescent detection of analytes in various fields.

Wenya Hou: Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wuyong Zhao: Methodology, Validation. Sijing Yi: Methodology, Validation. Wenmei Gao: Investigation, Validation. Jiangang Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Jinzhong Zhao: Project administration, Supervision. Xiaoxia Liu: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

The authors claim that none of the material in the paper has been published or is under consideration for publication elsewhere. No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and manuscript is approved by all authors for publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22002077) and the Science and Technology Innovation Foundation of Shanxi Agricultural University (2017YJ39, 2017YJ36).
